title: Unit 18 Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass: Passive Voice & Deponent Verbs (-ομαι)

script.text: Welcome... to the Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass for Unit 18: Deponent Verbs. Today, we tackle a group of verbs that are masters of disguise. They use Passive endings, but they have Active meanings. I'm William, and I'm joined by Athina. Athina... why do these verbs wear a 'Passive' mask?
Historically, these verbs usually describe things that happen 'to you' or 'within you'. Even if you are the one doing the action, there is an internal or reflexive element. We call them
Deponent. They have 'deposed' or set aside their active forms.
Set aside their active forms... interesting. So, even though they end in
we translate them as normal active verbs. Let’s look at the most common one: 'I am coming'.
which looks like passive. But you aren't 'being come'... you are just coming!
Again, a passive ending for a very active-feeling verb.
Now... Athina, many of these verbs are about emotions. Feelings that happen inside the soul.
Yes! This is the most natural group.
I fear. I am sorry. We are glad. You don't 'fear' something actively like you kick a ball. Fear is something that fills your state of being. That’s why the passive voice feels right to a Greek brain.
That’s a deep insight. What about physical states, like sleeping or wishing?
I sleep. I wish. I refuse. These reflect an internal decision or state of the body.
Pro Tip: When you see a verb ending in
don't immediately think 'is being done'. Check if it's one of our Deponent friends first. Athina, what happens when we put these into the Past or Future? Do they keep their passive disguise?
Yes! They stay in the passive track. The future of
They are rebels throughout their whole conjugation.
So if you've mastered Group B passive endings... you've already mastered the mechanics of Deponent verbs. You just have to remember their meanings are active.
Precisely. Don't be afraid of the
It’s just how some of our most important verbs like to talk.
Athina used a deponent verb right there. Excellent work today. We’ll see you in the next unit.
